name: Bean Nighe
description: The Bean Nighe, pronounced 'ben-nee-yeh' and meaning 'washing woman' in Scottish Gaelic, is a female spirit in Scottish folklore, considered a type of banshee. She is an omen of death, appearing to those who are about to die. Unlike some other death omens, the Bean Nighe is not necessarily malevolent; she is often portrayed as lamenting the impending demise of the individual. Her appearance is typically described as that of an old woman, often dressed in green, with long flowing hair. She is perpetually washing the grave-clothes of those about to die in a specific family line. To encounter her is considered a grave sign, as it means a close family member's death is imminent. While feared, her appearance provides a warning, allowing time for final farewells and preparations. Some accounts suggest she was once a woman who died in childbirth, and is now cursed to perform this task until the end of time, perpetually mourning the loss of her own child and the impending deaths of others.
subtitle: A Scottish spirit and omen of death.
appearance: Typically described as an old woman, often dressed in green or a grave shroud, with long flowing hair and webbed feet. Her eyes are often red from constant weeping.
habitat: Near lonely streams or pools where she washes the grave-clothes of those about to die.
behavior: She is often seen weeping and wailing while washing the grave-clothes. She is not actively malicious, but her appearance is a harbinger of grief and loss.

keyMyths.title: The Washing of the Shroud
keyMyths.description: A traveler stumbles upon the Bean Nighe washing a shroud. He realizes the meaning and rushes home to find his family grieving over his brother who had just taken ill and passed. The traveler was able to say a final goodbye, thanks to the warning.

keyMyths.title: Questioning the Washerwoman
keyMyths.description: A brave man encounters the Bean Nighe. Instead of fleeing, he directly questions her about whose clothes she is washing. Depending on the version of the story, she may reveal the name of the dying person or even grant the questioner a boon, though such interactions are fraught with danger.

keyMyths.title: The Bean Nighe and the Chieftain
keyMyths.description: A Highland chieftain is warned by the Bean Nighe of his impending death in battle. He heeds her warning and avoids the battle, saving his life and the lives of his clansmen, though he is forever haunted by the encounter.

sources.author: Campbell, John Gregorson
